text: The surgeon begins the lymph node dissection by identifying the ureter, obturator, umbilical artery, and vas deferens. The peritoneum overlying the external iliac vessels is incised to expose the vas deferens, followed by lateral incision near the obliterated umbilical artery. Sharp dissection of areolar tissue on the lateral side of the ureter allows access to deeper layers, exposing the deep portion of the obliterated umbilical artery. The vas deferens is further exposed, and the peritoneum is incised along the obliterated umbilical artery. The dissection proceeds to the adventitia of the external iliac artery, sharply incising areolar tissue to expose the proximal part of the artery. Dissection continues towards the deep inguinal ring, preserving a nerve structure by pushing it away from the lymph node packet, and detaching it from the psoas muscle.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lymph node packet from the external iliac artery, cauterizing an overlying vessel before sharply dissecting. A feeding vessel to the lymph node packet is skeletonized and secured with a hemi-lock before excision using CoTRI. The lymph node packet is mobilized under the external artery, allowing it to be grasped from the other side. The dissection continues under the artery, freeing the lymph node packet from the proximal part of the artery and muscle. The surgeon then performs a deep inguinal lymph node dissection, removing the lymph node packet from the distal portion of the external iliac artery and vein. The dissection progresses towards the deep inguinal ring, joining the plane from the earlier superficial inguinal lymph node dissection, marked by a clip placed during the open procedure. The surgeon identifies a small vein above the external iliac vein, marking the dissection extent, and continues under this vein to find the muscle belly.

The surgeon performs an obturator lymph node dissection, carefully dissecting the lymph node packet from the external iliac vein using cautery. Adequate counter traction is applied to expose the areola tissue for sharp incision. The dissection proceeds from medial to lateral around the obturator muscle, identifying the obturator nerve and artery. Blunt dissection is used near the nerve to avoid stimulation, completing the obturator dissection.
